items.prompt_md: In the jump-game reachability greedy, what single value do you track across the scan?
options: The farthest index reachable so far
The exact sequence of jumps taken to get here
The smallest step value seen so far
A sorted list of every index that's reachable
items.answer: The farthest index reachable so far
items.explanation_md: Tracking the farthest reach alone is enough because it dominates every smaller reachable index — you never need to know how you got there.

items.prompt_md: Why is it safe to forget which earlier index produced the current farthest reach?
options: Reachable indices always form one contiguous prefix [0, farthest], so any earlier reachable index is dominated by farthest
Because the array is guaranteed to be sorted
Because only the last index in the array matters
Because jump lengths are always equal to each other
items.answer: Reachable indices always form one contiguous prefix [0, farthest], so any earlier reachable index is dominated by farthest
items.explanation_md: If an earlier index k is reachable, everything between k and farthest is too — so k's identity is discarded information once farthest is known.

items.prompt_md: What check, done at index i before updating farthest, proves the end is unreachable?
options: i > farthest
nums[i] == 0
i == len(nums) - 1
farthest == 0
items.answer: i > farthest
items.explanation_md: If i is already past everything reachable so far, nothing earlier could have jumped over that gap either, so the trip is over.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the one-pass farthest-reach scan over n positions?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Each position is visited exactly once, doing O(1) work per position (one comparison, one max update).

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the greedy farthest-reach approach?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Only a single running integer (farthest) is kept — no per-index table is ever built.

items.prompt_md: A DP formulation stores reachable[i] for every index. Why can a naive version of this degrade toward O(n^2)?
options: Checking 'is there any earlier reachable j that can jump to i' from scratch at each i re-scans prior indices instead of reusing one running value
DP tables always require quadratic space regardless of implementation
Arrays cannot be indexed in O(1) time in general
DP requires sorting the input first, which costs O(n log n)
items.answer: Checking 'is there any earlier reachable j that can jump to i' from scratch at each i re-scans prior indices instead of reusing one running value
items.explanation_md: Without collapsing the table into a running maximum, each index's check can re-examine all prior indices, stacking up to quadratic work.

items.prompt_md: What is the exchange-argument justification for discarding every reachable index except the farthest?
options: From any reachable index k < farthest, you could always choose to under-jump, so k never lets you reach anywhere farthest doesn't already cover
Indices are interchangeable because the array is sorted
Because nums[i] is always non-negative, position never matters
Because jump lengths sum to a fixed constant
items.answer: From any reachable index k < farthest, you could always choose to under-jump, so k never lets you reach anywhere farthest doesn't already cover
items.explanation_md: Swapping your 'real' launch point to k instead of the one that produced farthest can never extend the reach beyond what farthest already represents.

items.prompt_md: A buggy implementation updates farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]) before checking whether i > farthest. What goes wrong?
options: It can silently extend the reach using an index that was never actually reachable, giving a false positive
It causes an off-by-one error only in the final return value
It makes the algorithm run in O(n^2) instead of O(n)
Nothing — the order of these two steps never matters
items.answer: It can silently extend the reach using an index that was never actually reachable, giving a false positive
items.explanation_md: Updating farthest with an unreachable index's jump treats it as if it were a legitimate launch point, corrupting the result.

items.prompt_md: Why doesn't this pattern need to sort the input array first?
options: Position in the array is meaningful (it's the index you jump from), so reordering would destroy the problem
Sorting jump-length arrays is mathematically undefined
The array is already guaranteed sorted by the problem statement
Sorting always costs more than O(n^2) for integer arrays
items.answer: Position in the array is meaningful (it's the index you jump from), so reordering would destroy the problem
items.explanation_md: Unlike sort-then-pick greedy, here the array's existing left-to-right order IS the structure being traversed.

items.prompt_md: What's true about applying this same farthest-reach idea to the 'minimum number of jumps' variant instead of a yes/no answer?
options: The core farthest-reach tracking still applies, but you must additionally track jump boundaries and a jump count, not just a single reach value
It cannot be adapted at all — a completely different algorithm family is required
It only works if the array is first sorted in descending order
It becomes O(n^2) once you need a jump count instead of yes/no
items.answer: The core farthest-reach tracking still applies, but you must additionally track jump boundaries and a jump count, not just a single reach value
items.explanation_md: The same one-pass scan generalizes: you still track the farthest reach, but you also mark when the current jump's range is exhausted to increment a counter.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of a brute-force approach that recursively tries every possible sequence of jumps from each index?
options: Exponential, since each position can branch into multiple choices of how far to jump
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: Exponential, since each position can branch into multiple choices of how far to jump
items.explanation_md: Without the domination insight, every index offers multiple jump-length choices, and the branching compounds combinatorially.

items.prompt_md: Which phrase is the precise trigger for reaching into this pattern before writing any code?
options: Per-position max-step capacity, can you reach the far end?
Sort the array, then greedily pick the best remaining item
Find the shortest path in a weighted graph
Two pointers converge from both ends of a sorted array
items.answer: Per-position max-step capacity, can you reach the far end?
items.explanation_md: That shape — a per-position reach limit plus a yes/no reachability question — is the tell for tracking a single running farthest index.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array where nums[i] is the maximum number of steps you can jump forward from index i. You need a yes/no answer: can you reach the last index?
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Dynamic programming over subsequence lengths
Two pointers converging from both ends
Backtracking through every possible jump sequence
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: Per-position max-step capacity plus a reachability question is the classic greedy-reachability trigger; the farthest reach dominates all other reachable indices.

items.prompt_md: Given non-negative jump capacities per index, determine whether you can ever reach an index at or beyond a target position.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Sort the jump capacities before scanning
Use a min-heap of jump capacities
Recursively branch on every jump choice
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: Position order is the structure here, so sorting would destroy it; one running farthest-reach value answers the question directly.

items.prompt_md: You're told each index has a max forward reach, and you want the single farthest position reachable from the start, accounting for every intermediate index that's reachable along the way.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Build a DP table indexed by every position
Use two pointers moving toward each other
Sort by reach capacity, then binary search
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: Every earlier reachable index is dominated by the running farthest value, so one forward scan replaces a per-index table.

items.prompt_md: A robot at position 0 can move forward up to nums[i] steps from position i. Determine whether it can ever step past the end of the array.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Simulate every possible sequence of moves with backtracking
Use a stack to track visited positions
Sort positions by reach, largest first
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: This is the same reachability shape: track the running farthest reach and compare it against the target boundary.

items.prompt_md: You suspect a DP table indexed by position would answer a reachability question correctly, but it feels like overkill for an array of per-position max jump lengths.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Keep the DP table — no simpler approach exists
Use a heap keyed by jump length
Two pointers scanning from both ends
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: Every index the DP table would mark reachable is already dominated by the running farthest value, so the whole table collapses to one variable.

items.prompt_md: Each element of an array gives the maximum forward jump from that position. You want the smallest amount of extra memory — beyond a couple of scalars — needed to test yes/no reachability of the last index.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Store a boolean reachability flag for every index
Sort the array and binary search for the answer
Use recursion with memoization over all positions
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: O(1) space is achievable precisely because reachability only ever needs the single dominating farthest-reach value.

items.prompt_md: While scanning an array of per-position jump capacities left to right, the current index number exceeds the running farthest-reach value you've maintained so far. What should you conclude, and how did you get there?
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass — and conclude the end is unreachable
Recompute reachability for every index from a fresh DP array
Use a min-heap to find the minimum jump length remaining
Use two pointers to check the array for symmetry
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass — and conclude the end is unreachable
items.explanation_md: Once the scan index passes the running farthest reach, no earlier position could have jumped over that gap either — the trip is provably dead.

items.prompt_md: You have a list of meeting intervals (start, end) and must find the minimum number of meeting rooms required so no two overlapping meetings share a room.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Sort by start time and use a min-heap of end times to track how many meetings are simultaneously active
Reverse the array and scan backward
Two pointers moving toward the center of the sorted array
items.answer: Sort by start time and use a min-heap of end times to track how many meetings are simultaneously active
items.explanation_md: This is a scheduling/overlap-counting problem, not a reachability one — the count of active meetings can rise and fall, which needs a heap, not a single monotonic farthest-reach scalar.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array of integers, find two numbers that add up exactly to a target value.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Use two pointers starting at both ends, moving inward based on how the current sum compares to the target
Dynamic programming over subset sums
Backtracking over every possible pair
items.answer: Use two pointers starting at both ends, moving inward based on how the current sum compares to the target
items.explanation_md: There's no per-position jump capacity here — a sorted-array pair-sum search is the classic two-pointers trigger, and the reachability greedy simply doesn't apply.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether a string of parentheses is balanced — every open bracket has a matching close in the correct nested order.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Push opens onto a stack and pop on each matching close
Sort the characters first, then scan
Two pointers scanning from both ends of the string
items.answer: Push opens onto a stack and pop on each matching close
items.explanation_md: Nested matching needs a stack to remember which opens are still unmatched; a single running farthest-reach number can't represent nesting depth.

items.prompt_md: Given per-position max-jump capacities, you want the simplest possible test — no per-index table, just a couple of running scalars — for whether the last index is reachable.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Build a reachability table with one boolean per index
Sort the array descending by jump capacity
Use a min-heap of remaining jump capacities
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: The whole per-index table collapses into one running maximum because every smaller reachable index is dominated by it.

items.prompt_md: An array gives per-position jump capacities. You want to know if the last index is reachable, and you want the algorithm to run in a single left-to-right pass with no revisits.
options: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
Try every jump length from every index with recursion
Sort positions by capacity, then binary search
Use a stack of previously visited positions
items.answer: Track the farthest reachable index in one pass
items.explanation_md: One forward scan updating a running farthest value is exactly what 'no revisits, single pass' calls for in this reachability shape.
